Core practice: Clarify the capabilities, risks, or ethical constructs that the benchmark claims to test, and explain how these are operationalized into tasks.
Problem fixed: Vague terminology; weak construct validity
Benchmark instances (master): PsycoLLM / Psychometrics / PsychCounsel / ValueBench (survey-mentioned psychology/value benchmarks) — This tool converts standardized items from established psychological scales (e.g., the PHQ-9 depression screening scale, the GAD-7 anxiety scale) into structured LLM response prompts. | MoralBench / MIC (survey-mentioned value benchmarks) — MoralBench implements a structured preference elicitation protocol where LLM responses to moral dilemmas are scored not only on binary correctness but along orthogonal moral dimension weights.
Survey / methodology refs: BetterBench 2024; Raji et al. 2021; Can We Trust AI Benchmarks
Mini case: In a mental health hotline dialogue dataset, the model is constrained to generate a structured response aligned with PHQ-9 Item 3, confirming the user's perception and offering evidence-based support options, thereby operationalizing the construct of 'little interest or pleasure in doing things' into a clear, actionable task.

Core practice: Extract principles, duties, prohibited actions, and applicable scenarios from law, policy, ethical theory, and professional codes of conduct.
Problem fixed: Unclear source of ethical judgment; rubric difficult to justify.
Benchmark instances (master): ETHICS (2023) — ETHICS constructs an explicit annotation schema mapped to five major normative ethical traditions (care ethics, justice ethics, deontology, virtue ethics, commo)
Survey / methodology refs: AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF); Professional ethics, relevant laws and policies; Model Cards/Datasheets approach
Mini case: In the medical AI consultation dialogue dataset, this schema can be reused to relabel decision recommendations in physician–patient interactions (e.g., “whether to disclose a terminal diagnosis”) along three ethical axes: deontology (physician duties), care ethics (patient psychological well-being), and justice ethics (fairness in resource allocation), thereby evaluating model response consistency under varying ethical weightings.

Core practice: Split the evaluation into multiple scenarios and multiple metrics, rather than a single overall score.
Problem fixed: Single-score masking of diverse risks and trade-offs
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: HELM (2023); AI RMF (2023); Model Cards 2019
Mini case: A language model is evaluated on its ability to handle customer service queries. The evaluation is split into scenarios such as billing issues, technical support, and product inquiries, with metrics including response accuracy, empathy, and compliance with company policies.

Core practice: Generate tests using a capability × test-type system, rather than relying solely on held-out accuracy.
Problem fixed: Ordinary accuracy overestimates capability and makes it difficult to pinpoint specific bugs.
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: CheckList (Ribeiro et al., 2020); BetterBench 2024
Mini case: A company uses a Behavioral Testing Matrix to evaluate an LLM's performance in generating safe and reliable responses. By systematically testing different capabilities and scenarios, they identify and fix specific bugs, improving the model's overall reliability and trustworthiness.

Core practice: Test bias under both information-deficient and information-sufficient contexts.
Problem fixed: Tests only surface-level stereotypes and cannot distinguish between uninformed random guessing and bias that overrides factual information.
Benchmark instances (master): BBQ (2022) — BBQ constructs paired contextual templates: for each social dimension (e.g., religion, race), the same core biased proposition is embedded into two types of con
Survey / methodology refs: BBQ | Master instances: BBQ (2022); BetterBench 2024; Raji et al. 2021
Mini case: In the Chinese customer service dialogue dataset, ambiguous contexts (e.g., “This elderly customer repeatedly questions the refund process”) and disambiguating contexts (e.g., “This 72-year-old retired teacher repeatedly confirms details due to visual impairment preventing clear reading of the terms”) can be constructed from user complaint texts, while keeping the question “Who is more likely to be unreasonable?” and its answer options unchanged, to detect model sensitivity to age-based stereotypes.

Core practice: Localize existing benchmarks rather than directly translating them.
Problem fixed: Cross-cultural migration distortion
Benchmark instances (master): KoBBQ (2024) — A systematic cultural adaptation methodology: First, apply “cultural suitability filtering” to the original BBQ template—removing approximately 70% of items uns | CBBQ (2024) — Four-stage human-AI co-authored ambiguous/disambiguated QA template pipeline | NaVAB (2025) — Cross-national news-based value elicitation framework with expert-annotated multi-label consensus scoring
Survey / methodology refs: KoBBQ, CBBQ, NaVAB, etc. | Master instances: KoBBQ (2024); CBBQ (2024); NaVAB (2025); BetterBench 2024; Raji et al. 2021
Mini case: Constructing a dataset for AI assistants in Japanese workplaces using the KoBBQ framework: first applying “Japanese cultural filtering” to the Japanese translation of BBQ (removing questions unrelated to Japan’s lifetime employment system and seniority-based wage/promotion system), then augmenting with dimensions from Japan’s Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare’s White Paper on Workplace Discrimination.

Core practice: Use manual templates to ensure directionality; use models to scale up samples in bulk.
Problem fixed: Manual question generation is slow; pure model-based question generation yields unstable quality.
Benchmark instances (master): CBBQ (2024) — A four-stage human-AI collaborative construction process: (1) AI-based initial screening of raw texts containing social attribute controversies from CNKI and so
Survey / methodology refs: CBBQ (2024); BetterBench 2024; Raji et al. 2021
Mini case: In an AI-assisted question-generation system for training grassroots civil servants in China, the CBBQ four-stage process is adopted: AI extracts ambiguous statements concerning topics such as “employment of persons with disabilities” and “staffing quotas for rural-hukou teachers” from government bulletins and petition platforms; these statements are then disambiguated by human resources and social security experts to generate policy-comprehension multiple-choice questions (MCQs), which are used.

Core practice: Design both safe but easily rejected prompts and genuinely unsafe prompts simultaneously.
Problem fixed: Measures only refusal rate, ignoring over-refusal.
Benchmark instances (master): XSTest (2024) — This tool constructs rigorously paired prompt contrast sets: each pair comprises one semantically safe but easily over-refused prompt (e.g., expressing routine
Survey / methodology refs: XSTest | Master instances: XSTest (2024)
Mini case: In a medical Q&A dataset, for each real patient query (e.g., “How to relieve migraine”), manually construct a semantically safe but lexically vague/metaphorical counterpart (e.g., “What can I do to make my head quiet down, like turning off an old, buzzing air conditioner?”), forming contrastive pairs to evaluate whether medical large language models erroneously reject legitimate health inquiries due to terminological uncertainty.

Core practice: Categorize safety risks into multiple hazard types and establish response criteria.
Problem fixed: Security risks are overly broad, making structured assessment infeasible.
Benchmark instances (master): HarmBench (2024) — HarmBench constructs its test behaviors by systematically mapping 7 semantic harm categories (e.g., chemical weapons, non-consensual imagery, fraud) to real-world red-line vs. disputed-behavior labeling.
Survey / methodology refs: AILuminate; HarmBench (2024)
Mini case: In the financial risk control dialogue dataset, reconstruct HarmBench’s taxonomy based on China’s Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services and the CBIRC’s anti-money laundering guidelines: label 'forging bank statements' as red-line (explicitly illegal), and label 'teaching users reasonable tax planning' as disputed; then re-annotate 1,000 customer service dialogues using this labeling scheme to drive the model to learn how to provide ambiguous responses.

Core practice: Determine the appropriateness of information flow based on role, information type, purpose, and transmission principles.
Problem fixed: Privacy is narrowly defined as PII, ignoring broader contextual factors.
Benchmark instances (master): PrivaCI-Bench (2025) — This tool is grounded in legal statutes including the GDPR, HIPAA, and the EU AI Act, anchoring specific provisions (e.g., GDPR Article 6 on lawfulness bases; HIPAA Privacy Rule).
Survey / methodology refs: PrivaCI-Bench (2025); Privacy Theory; Nissenbaum’s Contextual Integrity (CI) theory
Mini case: In a financial customer service dataset, this tool aligns with GLBA Section 501 security standards, generating MCQs like 'The agent stores this information in an unencrypted database—violating which GLBA requirement?' with a data flow diagram for context.

Core practice: Flag variables that appear neutral but may proxy for protected attributes.
Problem fixed: The fairness benchmark only measures explicit bias and overlooks indirect discrimination.
Benchmark instances (master): BBQ (2022) — BBQ constructs paired contextual templates: for each social dimension (e.g., religion, race), the same core biased proposition is embedded into two types of con | CBBQ (2024) — A four-stage human-AI collaborative construction process: (1) AI-based initial screening of raw texts containing social attribute controversies from CNKI and so
Survey / methodology refs: Fair ML and anti-discrimination literature; BBQ/CBBQ adaptation
Mini case: In a Chinese customer service dialogue dataset, ambiguous contexts (e.g., 'This elderly customer repeatedly questions the refund process') and disambiguating contexts (e.g., 'This 72-year-old retired teacher repeatedly confirms details due to visual impairment preventing clear reading of the terms') are constructed to detect model sensitivity to age-based stereotypes.

Core practice: Categorize requests or scenarios as low-, medium-, or high-risk based on risk intensity.
Problem fixed: One-size-fits-all risk assessment leads to excessive rejection or underestimation of crises.
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: Expert-recommended (gap-driven; no benchmark instance in master corpus). Safety benchmark, crisis intervention scenario
Mini case: In a healthcare AI system, patient requests are categorized into low, medium, and high risk. Low-risk requests (e.g., routine check-ups) are handled by chatbots, while high-risk requests (e.g., severe symptoms) are escalated to human doctors, ensuring appropriate and timely care.

Core practice: Specify that the model must not overstep its authority in medical, legal, psychological, and other domains.
Problem fixed: Empathy/Professional Advice Benchmark is prone to overreach.
Benchmark instances (master): ETHICS (2023) — ETHICS constructs an explicit annotation schema mapped to five major normative ethical traditions (care ethics, justice ethics, deontology, virtue ethics, commo)
Survey / methodology refs: Professional ethics; psychological/medical/legal norms; ETHICS (2023)
Mini case: In a medical AI consultation dialogue dataset, this schema can be reused to relabel decision recommendations in physician–patient interactions along three ethical axes: deontology, care ethics, and justice ethics, evaluating model response consistency under varying ethical weightings.

Core practice: Testing boundary stability through multi-round induction, role-playing, and request packaging.
Problem fixed: Single-turn safety questions cannot detect boundary drift.
Benchmark instances (master): HarmBench (2024) — HarmBench constructs its test behaviors by systematically mapping 7 semantic harm categories to real-world policies with explicit red-line vs. disputed-behavior labeling. | ToxicChat (2023) — This tool captures real first-turn user prompts from the Vicuna online demo and manually or semi-automatically labels whether the subsequent AI response is toxic.
Survey / methodology refs: HarmBench/JailbreakBench/ToxicChat, etc. | Master instances: HarmBench (2024); ToxicChat (2023); Raji et al. 2021: Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing; AI RMF: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
Mini case: In a financial risk control dialogue dataset, reconstruct HarmBench’s taxonomy based on China’s Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services and the CBIRC’s anti-money laundering guidelines. Label 'forging bank statements' as red-line (explicitly illegal) and 'teaching users reasonable tax planning' as disputed, then re-annotate 1,000 customer service dialogues using this labeling scheme.

Core practice: Test model actions in environments such as tool invocation, file systems, email, and databases.
Problem fixed: Text-response safety does not equal action safety.
Benchmark instances (master): Agent-SafetyBench (2025) — This tool is a test case generation framework designed for LLM agents: it constructs 2,000 multi-turn interaction trajectories in the ToolEmu sandbox environment.
Survey / methodology refs: Agent-SafetyBench, ToolEmu, etc. | Master instances: Agent-SafetyBench (2025); BetterBench 2024; Raji et al. 2021
Mini case: In the Intelligent Office Assistant dataset, generate multi-turn test cases based on the 'Email Auto-Archiving' functionality. The user requests to move emails, and the agent invokes the search API. In the second turn, the agent simulates an API failure to observe if it executes the move operation without secondary verification, evaluating its robustness.

Canonical name: Large Language Model Scorer and Guidelines
Core practice: Evaluate open-ended responses using strong models or multi-model ensembles, and reduce bias through rubrics and human calibration.
Problem fixed: Open-ended responses are difficult to score automatically.
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: MT-Bench; Chatbot Arena; LLM-as-Judge survey
Mini case: In an educational setting, LLM-as-a-judge is used to evaluate student essays. The tool employs a strong language model to score the essays, and a detailed rubric is provided to ensure consistent and fair evaluations. Human calibration is used to further reduce bias and improve the reliability of the scores.

Core practice: Multiple judges or human-model collaboration for scoring, reducing bias from a single evaluator.
Problem fixed: Single LLM judge is unstable.
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: Expert-recommended (gap-driven; no benchmark instance in master corpus). LLM judge-related research
Mini case: In an ethical judgment task, multiple human evaluators and LLMs collaboratively score responses, reducing the variability and bias that a single LLM might introduce.

Canonical name: Public Practice and Hidden Test Data
Core practice: Publish practice data; keep formal test data hidden.
Problem fixed: Data contamination, leaderboard gaming, Goodhart’s law
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: AILuminate; MLCommons-style benchmarking practices
Mini case: A machine learning competition uses a public dataset for participants to train their models. A separate, hidden dataset is used for final evaluation, ensuring that the top-performing models have not overfitted to the training data.

Core practice: Update the question set in response to evolving model capabilities, legal requirements, and societal risks, while maintaining version control.
Problem fixed: Benchmark outdated, difficult to compare
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: BetterBench 2024; AI RMF
Mini case: A benchmark for evaluating AI models in healthcare is updated annually to reflect new medical guidelines, emerging diseases, and changes in data privacy laws, ensuring the benchmark remains relevant and reliable.

Core practice: Intended use, out-of-scope, data sources, limitations, maintenance approach
Problem fixed: User misuse of benchmark; insufficient documentation
Benchmark instances (master): (no master instance — see survey refs)
Survey / methodology refs: Datasheets (Gebru et al., 2018); Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019); Data Cards (Holland et al., 2018); BetterBench (2024)
Mini case: A research team releases a new benchmark for evaluating AI models in healthcare. They create a detailed Benchmark Card, outlining the intended use, data sources, limitations, and maintenance plan, ensuring that users can apply the benchmark appropriately and understand its context.
